title: Email notifications arriving hours late
body: Task assignment notifications used to arrive within a minute or two. For the past week, they've been arriving 2-4 hours late. This is causing people to miss deadlines because they don't know they've been assigned a task until hours later. It seems to be worse in the morning — notifications sent around 9-10am take the longest to arrive. Afternoon notifications are usually faster but still delayed.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed report — the morning pattern is a really useful clue.

To help narrow this down: is this affecting all users on your team, or only some?

- **All users** — everyone sees the delays
- **Specific users** — only certain people experience it (e.g., certain email domains or roles)
- **Not sure** — you've only confirmed it on your own account

This will help us figure out whether we're looking at a system-wide infrastructure issue or something more targeted.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: It's affecting all users as far as I can tell. I've heard from at least 3 or 4 other people on my team complaining about the same thing — late assignment notifications, especially in the morning. We're all on the same company email domain so I can't speak to whether other domains would be affected. But it definitely seems system-wide, not just me.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's really helpful — system-wide plus the morning pattern points toward an infrastructure issue rather than anything account-specific.

The next thing that would help us most is understanding *where* the delay is happening. Could you check the email headers on one of the late notifications? Most email clients let you view headers (in Gmail: open the email → three dots → "Show original"). We're looking for:

- **The earliest timestamp** in the headers (when TaskFlow handed the email off) vs. **when it arrived** — if there's a big gap right at the start, the delay is in TaskFlow's queue before sending.
- **A gap between two middle hops** — that would point to a delay in the email delivery infrastructure.

If checking headers isn't practical, do you happen to know whether your team's email setup changed recently (e.g., new spam filtering, email gateway, or security appliance in the last couple of weeks)?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
I'm not super familiar with reading email headers honestly, but I did take a quick look at one of the delayed notifications in Gmail. The timestamps seemed to show the delay was early on — like there was a big gap between when I'd expect TaskFlow to send it and when it actually got handed off. So I think the holdup is on TaskFlow's side, not our email system.

As far as I know, nothing changed with our email setup recently. But I did notice that about a week ago — which is roughly when this started — we started getting these new daily digest emails from TaskFlow. Some kind of marketing summary thing? Not sure if that's related but the timing lines up.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Email notification delays (2-4 hours) caused by likely queue saturation from new daily digest feature","problem":"Task assignment email notifications that previously arrived within 1-2 minutes are now delayed 2-4 hours. The delays are system-wide, affecting all users, and are worst in the morning (9-10am sends take the longest). The issue started approximately one week ago.","root_cause_hypothesis":"The new daily digest email feature, introduced ~1 week ago, is likely saturating TaskFlow's email sending queue. Digest emails are probably sent in bulk each morning, flooding the queue and causing transactional notifications (like task assignments) to wait behind thousands of digest emails. This explains both the timing correlation and the morning-heavy delay pattern.","reproduction_steps":["Assign a task to a user in the morning (9-10am window)","Observe the email notification delivery time — expect 2-4 hour delay","Compare with the email send queue depth at that time","Check whether the daily digest batch job runs just before the observed delay window"],"environment":"System-wide, all users on at least one company email domain. Gmail confirmed as one affected client. Production environment.","severity":"high","impact":"All users are receiving late task assignment notifications, causing missed deadlines. This undermines the core value proposition of task assignment notifications.","recommended_fix":"1. Confirm the hypothesis by checking the email queue depth and digest send times vs. notification send times in the past week. 2. Separate transactional emails (notifications) from bulk emails (digests) into distinct queues or sending channels, with transactional emails given priority. 3. Consider rate-limiting or staggering digest sends to avoid queue saturation. 4. As a quick mitigation, move digest sends to an off-peak window (e.g., early morning before business hours).","proposed_test_case":"After the fix, assign a task at 9:30am (peak delay window) and verify the notification email arrives within 2 minutes. Additionally, confirm that daily digests still deliver successfully, just without blocking transactional emails.","information_gaps":["Exact email queue implementation (single queue vs. multiple) — requires codebase inspection","Precise schedule and volume of the daily digest sends — requires checking the batch job configuration","Whether other notification types (not just assignments) are also delayed — reporter only mentioned assignments"]}
